section.title: David, Nabal, and Abigail
section.sectionRationale: David's men have protected Nabal's flocks in the wilderness, but when David asks for a share of the feast, Nabal repays kindness with insult, living up to his name, Fool. David straps on his sword to wipe out every male in Nabal's household, but Nabal's wife Abigail rides out with provisions and wisdom, restraining David from bloodguilt and vengeance. The LORD Himself later strikes Nabal dead, and David takes Abigail as his wife. It is one movement: the same lesson as the Saul episodes, leave vengeance to the LORD.
section.thesis: When Nabal's folly and insult provoke David to take vengeance and shed blood with his own hand, Abigail's wise intervention restrains him from bloodguilt, and the LORD Himself repays Nabal's wickedness, teaching David again that vindication belongs to God, not to the sword.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Nabal repays kindness with contempt, verses 1 to 13; Abigail acts, verses 14 to 19; Abigail's plea to David, verses 20 to 31; David blesses her discernment, verses 32 to 35; and the LORD strikes Nabal, and David marries Abigail, verses 36 to 44.

verses.text: When Samuel died, all Israel gathered to mourn for him; and they buried him at his home in Ramah. Then David set out and went down to the Wilderness of Paran.
Now there was a man in Maon whose business was in Carmel. He was a very wealthy man with a thousand goats and three thousand sheep, which he was shearing in Carmel.
His name was Nabal, and his wife's name was Abigail. She was an intelligent and beautiful woman, but her husband, a Calebite, was harsh and evil in his dealings.
While David was in the wilderness, he heard that Nabal was shearing sheep.
So David sent ten young men and instructed them, "Go up to Nabal at Carmel. Greet him in my name
and say to him, 'Long life to you, and peace to you and to your house and to all that belongs to you.
Now I hear that it is time for shearing. When your shepherds were with us, we did not harass them, and nothing of theirs was missing the whole time they were in Carmel.

Ask your young men, and they will tell you. So let my young men find favor with you, for we have come on the day of a feast. Please give whatever you can spare to your servants and to your son David.'"
When David's young men arrived, they relayed all these words to Nabal on behalf of David. Then they waited.
But Nabal asked them, "Who is David? Who is this son of Jesse? Many servants these days are breaking away from their masters.
Why should I take my bread and water and the meat I have slaughtered for my shearers, and give them to these men whose origin I do not know?"
So David's men turned around and went back, and they relayed to him all these words.
And David said to his men, "Strap on your swords!" So David and all his men strapped on their swords, and about four hundred men followed David, while two hundred stayed with the supplies.

units.label: Abigail acts
units.note: she did not tell her husband

verses.text: Meanwhile, one of Nabal's young men informed Nabal's wife Abigail, "Look, David sent messengers from the wilderness to greet our master, but he screamed at them.
Yet these men were very good to us. When we were in the field, we were not harassed, and nothing of ours went missing the whole time we lived among them.
They were a wall around us, both day and night, the whole time we were herding our sheep near them.
Now consider carefully what you must do, because disaster looms over our master and all his household. For he is such a scoundrel that nobody can speak to him!"

Then Abigail hurried and took two hundred loaves of bread, two skins of wine, five butchered sheep, five seahs of roasted grain, a hundred clusters of raisins, and two hundred cakes of figs. She loaded them on donkeys
and said to her young men, "Go ahead of me. I will be right behind you." But she did not tell her husband Nabal.
As Abigail came riding her donkey into a mountain ravine, she saw David and his men coming down toward her, and she met them.
Now David had just said, "In vain I have protected all that belonged to this man in the wilderness. Nothing that belongs to him has gone missing, yet he has paid me back evil for good.
May God punish David, and ever so severely, if I let one male belonging to Nabal survive until morning."
When Abigail saw David, she quickly got off the donkey, fell facedown, and bowed before him.
She fell at his feet and said, "My lord, may the blame be on me alone, but please let your servant speak to you; hear the words of your servant.

My lord should pay no attention to this scoundrel {{nabal|Nabal}}, for he lives up to his name: His name means Fool, and {{nabal|folly}} accompanies him. I, your servant, did not see my lord's young men whom you sent.

Now, my lord, as surely as the LORD lives and you yourself live, since the LORD has held you back from {{dam|bloodshed}} and from avenging yourself with your own hand, may your enemies and those who seek harm for my lord be like Nabal.
Now let this gift your servant has brought to my lord be given to the young men who follow you.
Please forgive your servant's offense, for the LORD will surely make a lasting dynasty for my lord, because he fights the LORD's battles. May no evil be found in you as long as you live.

And should someone pursue you and seek your life, then the life of my lord will be bound securely by the LORD your God in the bundle of the living. But He shall fling away the lives of your enemies like stones from a sling.
When the LORD has done for my lord all the good He promised, and when He has appointed you ruler over Israel,
then my lord will have no remorse or guilt of conscience over needless {{dam|bloodshed}} and revenge. And when the LORD has dealt well with my lord, may you remember your servant."
Then David said to Abigail, "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who sent you to meet me this day!
Blessed is your discernment, and blessed are you, because today you kept me from {{dam|bloodshed}} and from avenging myself by my own hand.

Otherwise, as surely as the LORD, the God of Israel, lives, who has restrained me from harming you, if you had not come quickly to meet me, then surely no male belonging to Nabal would have been left alive by morning light."
Then David accepted from her hand what she had brought him, and he said to her, "Go home in peace. See, I have heeded your voice and granted your request."

units.label: The LORD strikes Nabal, David marries Abigail
units.note: the LORD brought his wickedness down on his own head

verses.text: When Abigail returned to Nabal, there he was in the house, holding a feast fit for a king, in high spirits and very drunk. So she told him nothing until morning light.
In the morning when Nabal was sober, his wife told him about these events, and his heart failed within him, and he became like a stone.
About ten days later, the LORD struck Nabal dead.

On hearing that Nabal was dead, David said, "Blessed be the LORD, who has upheld my cause against the reproach of Nabal and has restrained His servant from evil. For the LORD has brought the wickedness of Nabal down upon his own head." Then David sent word to Abigail, asking her to become his wife.
When his servants came to Abigail at Carmel, they said, "David has sent us to take you as his wife."
She arose, bowed facedown, and said, "Here is your servant, ready to serve and to wash the feet of my lord's servants."
So Abigail hurried and got on a donkey, and attended by five of her maidens, she followed David's messengers and became his wife.
David had also married Ahinoam of Jezreel. So she and Abigail were both his wives.
But Saul had given his daughter Michal, David's wife, to Palti son of Laish, who was from Gallim.

nabal.gloss: Fool, senseless, morally obtuse. Abigail's wordplay in verse 25 makes explicit what the story has shown: Nabal lives up to his own name, a fool who cannot see the danger he has brought on himself by repaying kindness with contempt.

dam.gloss: Blood, and by extension bloodguilt, the guilt incurred by shedding blood, especially in unjustified vengeance. Abigail credits the LORD with holding David back from bloodshed, verse 26, and David himself blesses her for keeping him from it, verses 31 and 33, from taking vengeance into his own hands.

basic.context: David's men have kept Nabal's shepherds and flocks safe in the wilderness, expecting only ordinary hospitality in return at shearing time. Nabal, whose name means Fool, insults David's messengers and refuses to give anything. Furious, David straps on his sword to kill every male in Nabal's household by morning. But Nabal's wife Abigail, described as intelligent, quickly rides out with a generous gift and a wise, humble speech, persuading David to leave vengeance to the LORD. David blesses her for keeping him from bloodguilt. Ten days later the LORD Himself strikes Nabal dead, and David takes Abigail as his wife.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history that also teaches enduring wisdom: it is not a proof text about how every marriage or every insult should be handled, but it shows what it costs to let anger drive you toward vengeance, and what it looks like when a courageous, wise person steps in to prevent needless bloodshed. The key question is whether David will take justice into his own hands or trust God to repay wrong; Abigail becomes the means by which he chooses the better path.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on wordplay: Nabal is nabal, fool, and Abigail says plainly that folly, nabal, accompanies him, verse 25. And three times the chapter uses dam, blood, to describe what Abigail and then David are glad was avoided, needless bloodshed and vengeance taken by David's own hand, verses 26, 31, and 33.
basic.typology: Abigail's intervention, bearing a gift and interceding to turn away deserved wrath and avert bloodshed, offers a picture of the intercessor who stands between deserved judgment and its object; ultimately Christ intercedes for His people and turns away the wrath they deserve, though unlike Abigail He does so by bearing the judgment Himself.
basic.christ: David, God's anointed, is here kept by another's wise intervention from doing to Nabal's household what would have stained his hands with needless blood, just as in the Saul episodes he is kept from avenging himself. This restraint anticipates Christ, who, though possessing all right to judge, did not come to destroy sinners' lives but to give His own, and who intercedes to turn wrath away from those who deserve it, exactly what Abigail does for Nabal's household.
basic.god: The LORD is shown to be the one who truly repays wickedness in His own time and way; He does not need David's sword to settle accounts with Nabal, He strikes him Himself. God works through Abigail's wisdom and courage to keep His anointed from sin, showing that His providence often comes through the wise and faithful action of ordinary people.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 24 and 26 show David refusing to touch the LORD's anointed Saul, the same restraint from personal vengeance. Romans 12 tells believers to leave room for God's wrath rather than avenging themselves. Deuteronomy 32 says vengeance belongs to the LORD. Proverbs often warns that fools cannot be reasoned with, exactly as Nabal is portrayed.
basic.oneStory: This is another movement in David's long wait for the throne, in which God repeatedly teaches him to leave vengeance to the LORD rather than seizing it himself. Abigail's wisdom in turning away deserved wrath quietly foreshadows the far greater intercession of Christ, who turns away the wrath His people deserve and brings them, like Abigail, into covenant relationship with the anointed king.

context.literary: A carefully built story of insult, rising danger, and rescue: Nabal's contemptuous refusal, David's furious vow, Abigail's urgent and eloquent intervention, David's grateful blessing, and the LORD's own act of judgment that resolves the crisis and leads to marriage. The narrator's wordplay on Nabal's name signals the moral shape of the whole account from the start, verse 3.
context.historical: Shearing time was a customary occasion for generosity and feasting in ancient Israel, and shepherds who protected a flock in open country, as David's men had done for Nabal's, could reasonably expect hospitality in return, much as ancient Near Eastern custom recognized reciprocal obligations between protectors and those they protected. Nabal's wealth and harsh refusal make his insult to David, God's anointed and a public benefactor to his household, especially stark.
context.canonical: This chapter sits alongside the two cave-and-camp episodes with Saul, chapters 24 and 26, forming a pattern in which David is repeatedly restrained, by conscience, by Abigail, from taking vengeance into his own hands, so that his kingship is established as a gift from God rather than a prize won by personal violence.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how Abigail's wise, courageous intervention restrained David from shedding innocent blood in anger, and how the LORD Himself, not David's sword, brought just judgment on Nabal's folly, reinforcing the pattern that vengeance belongs to God alone.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific rescue from a specific act of near-vengeance; it does not prescribe polygamy, which Scripture elsewhere qualifies and which brought real trouble into David's household, nor does it hand out a formula for handling every insult. What endures is the principle that vengeance belongs to the LORD, and that wise intervention can prevent great harm.

debates.question: Was David's intention to kill Nabal's household justified self-defense against an insult, or sinful vengeance?

views.label: Sinful, excessive vengeance
views.case: David himself later thanks God and Abigail for keeping him from bloodguilt, verses 31 and 33, language that only makes sense if what he intended was genuinely sinful and would have brought guilt on him.

views.label: An understandable but still wrong overreaction
views.case: Nabal's insult was a real wrong against David's men who had faithfully protected his flocks, so David's anger is understandable, yet the scale of his response, killing an entire household over an insult, still went beyond justice into vengeance that the LORD and Abigail rightly restrained.

debates.bearing: Both views agree that David's planned action was wrong and that Abigail's intervention was a genuine act of God's mercy keeping him from sin, whatever sympathy we might have for his original grievance.

debates.question: How should we assess David's marriage to Abigail so soon after Nabal's death?

views.label: A providential match, honoring a worthy woman
views.case: Abigail's wisdom and courage marked her as an exceptional woman, and David's marrying her can be read as honoring her character and bringing her under his protection after she was widowed through no fault of her own.

views.label: An early sign of the polygamy that later troubled David's house
views.case: Verse 43 notes David had already married Ahinoam, and this second marriage begins the pattern of multiple wives that Scripture elsewhere shows brought significant strife into David's family; the narrative records it without commending polygamy as an ideal.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter's main point is not David's marriage practices but Abigail's role in restraining bloodshed; the marriage itself is reported, not prescribed, as with much of the patriarchal narratives.

study.originalLanguages: The story is built on a pun: Nabal is nabal, fool, and Abigail names it outright, verse 25, his name means Fool, and folly, nabal, goes with him, exposing that his contempt was not shrewdness but senseless self-destruction. The word dam, blood, appears three times, verses 26, 31, and 33, always describing the bloodshed and bloodguilt from which Abigail's intervention, and ultimately the LORD, kept David, showing that the chapter's central concern is not Nabal's rudeness but whether David's hands would be stained with unjustified killing.

typology.type: Abigail interceding with gifts to turn away deserved wrath from a guilty household
typology.fulfillment: Christ interceding for His people, turning away the wrath they deserve, though at the cost of bearing it Himself, Romans 8:34 and Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: David restrained from taking vengeance by his own hand
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who though He will judge the living and the dead, did not come at His first coming to destroy but to save, John 3:17 and Romans 12:19.

study.christ: David, already anointed but not yet enthroned, is again kept from sin by being restrained from personal vengeance, this time through Abigail's wise and humble intercession rather than his own conscience alone. Her gift and her plea turn away wrath that Nabal's household justly deserved, dimly foreshadowing Christ, our great Intercessor, who turns away the wrath His people deserve, though He does so not merely by pleading but by bearing that wrath Himself on the cross, Romans 8:34, Hebrews 7:25. As David was kept from shedding blood by another's intervention, so believers are kept from receiving the judgment they have earned because Christ has intervened for them completely.
study.god: God is the true Judge who repays wickedness in His own way and time; He does not require David's vengeance to deal with Nabal, He strikes him dead Himself, verse 38, ten days after the crisis, showing that patience in leaving room for God's justice is never patience wasted. God also works through the courage and discernment of an ordinary, faithful woman to accomplish His purposes and protect His anointed servant from sin, showing that His providence often comes wrapped in human wisdom and initiative.

crossReferences.note: David likewise refuses to harm the LORD's anointed Saul, sparing him rather than taking vengeance himself.
A second episode of David sparing Saul, the same restraint from personal vengeance reinforced.
Vengeance is Mine, and retribution, in due time their foot will slip, the principle Abigail urges on David.
Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave room for God's wrath, echoing the lesson David learns twice over from Saul and now from Nabal.

study.oneStory: God's anointed but still-waiting king is again kept from seizing vengeance with his own hand, this time through the wisdom of Abigail, and the LORD Himself repays the wicked in His own time. The pattern running through Saul's sparing and now Nabal's judgment shapes David into a king who receives both his throne and his justice from God's hand alone, a pattern completed in Christ, who intercedes for sinners and will Himself judge all wrong in the end.

quiz.q: Why did Nabal refuse David's request for provisions?

options.text: He dismissed David with contempt, living up to his name, Fool, despite the real protection David's men had given his flocks.
options.note: Verses 10-11 show Nabal's scornful refusal, and verse 25 explicitly ties his behavior to his name's meaning.

options.text: He genuinely did not have enough food to spare for anyone.
options.note: Nabal was very wealthy, verse 2, with abundant supplies for a feast; his refusal was about contempt, not scarcity.

options.text: He was following Saul's orders to withhold help from David.
options.note: No such order is mentioned; the text presents Nabal's refusal as his own harsh character, not a directive from Saul.

options.text: He did not receive David's message at all.
options.note: David's men delivered the message directly and Nabal responded to it in person, verses 9-11.

quiz.q: What did David intend to do after Nabal's insult, and who stopped him?

options.text: He intended to kill every male in Nabal's household, but Abigail's intervention restrained him.
options.note: Verses 13, 21-22, and 32-34 show David's intent and Abigail's decisive role in stopping it.

options.text: He intended only to take back the goods his men had left with Nabal's shepherds.
options.note: David's stated intent was far more severe, to kill Nabal's entire household, not merely reclaim goods.

options.text: He intended to report Nabal to Saul for judgment.
options.note: David planned direct violent action himself, not an appeal to Saul's court.

options.text: He had already forgiven Nabal before Abigail arrived.
options.note: David was still bent on vengeance until Abigail met him, verses 21-22 and 32-34.

quiz.q: How does Abigail explain Nabal's behavior in verse 25?

options.text: She says he lives up to his name, Fool, and that folly accompanies him.
options.note: Verse 25 makes the wordplay explicit, tying Nabal's conduct directly to the meaning of his name.

options.text: She blames David's messengers for provoking him.
options.note: She takes personal blame on herself, verse 24, but does not blame David's messengers; she names Nabal's own folly as the cause.

options.text: She claims Nabal was simply testing David's patience.
options.note: She presents his insult as genuine folly, not a deliberate test.

options.text: She says Nabal acted wisely given the circumstances.
options.note: She explicitly calls him a scoundrel and a fool, the opposite of commending his wisdom.

quiz.q: What does David bless Abigail for in verses 32-33?

options.text: For her discernment in keeping him from bloodshed and from avenging himself with his own hand.
options.note: David's words in verses 32-33 explicitly credit her with restraining him from bloodguilt and personal vengeance.

options.text: For providing enough food to feed his four hundred men indefinitely.
options.note: The gift was generous and welcome, but David's blessing centers on being kept from sin, not merely on the food supply.

options.text: For convincing him that Nabal deserved to live.
options.note: David does not conclude Nabal is innocent; his gratitude is for being kept from taking vengeance himself, not for a verdict on Nabal's guilt.

options.text: For predicting the exact day Nabal would die.
options.note: Abigail does not predict Nabal's death; the LORD strikes him about ten days later, unrelated to any prediction from her.

quiz.q: How did the LORD finally deal with Nabal, and what does this teach?

options.text: The LORD struck Nabal dead about ten days later, showing that vengeance belongs to God, not to David's own hand.
options.note: Verses 37-38 show God Himself executing judgment, vindicating Abigail's counsel to leave vengeance to Him.

options.text: David's men eventually returned and killed Nabal in secret.
options.note: The text is explicit that the LORD struck Nabal; no human hand from David's camp is involved in his death.

options.text: Nabal repented and made peace with David before he died.
options.note: Nabal's heart failed him in fear when he heard the news, but there is no report of repentance or reconciliation before his death.

options.text: Nabal lived on for many years unpunished.
options.note: The text specifies he died about ten days after the events, struck by the LORD.

questions: David's men had faithfully protected Nabal's flocks, yet Nabal repaid them with contempt. When has your own kindness been met with an ungrateful or hostile response, and how did you respond?
David was ready to shed blood over a personal insult until Abigail intervened. Where in your life might anger be pushing you toward a response far more severe than the situation calls for?
Abigail risked her own safety to speak hard truth and turn David from sin. Is there a moment where you need the courage to intervene wisely before someone you care about does lasting harm?

David later blessed God and Abigail for keeping him from bloodguilt rather than resenting the interruption to his plans. How do you typically respond to someone who stops you from a mistake, gratitude or irritation?
The LORD dealt with Nabal in His own time, without David lifting a hand. Where do you need to trust God's timing for justice rather than forcing a resolution yourself?

translationNotes.note: Calebite may describe Nabal's clan from Caleb's line in Judah, or the BSB footnote notes some read it as describing his temperament as dog-like or churlish; either way the narrator signals his harsh, evil dealings.

May God punish David renders an oath formula in which the speaker names himself in a curse he intends for someone else; some manuscripts read David's enemies instead, but the sense is the same self-imprecating oath over the planned slaughter.
The bundle of the living pictures a pouch in which precious items are securely bound and kept safe, a vivid image for how the LORD will guard David's life against his enemies.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 25:1-44. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none appear anywhere in this file.
